serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of black vinegar pig's trotter is approximately 250 g (one bowl), comprising trotter pieces with braising liquid, as commonly served in Singaporean households and postpartum confinement meals.

nutrient_source.title: Black Vinegar Pig's Trotter — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Black Vinegar Pig's Trotter (cr_id F06100005). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Pork Dishes with Vinegar and Sugar — Analogous Estimate Based on University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for black vinegar pig's trotter in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on analogous braised pork dishes with added sugar and vinegar. The acetic acid in black vinegar is well-documented to attenuate postprandial glucose response (Liljeberg & Björck, 1998, European Journal of Clinical Nutrition), and the moderate sugar content (8.9 g/100g) is partially offset by the vinegar and protein matrix. GL per 250 g serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per serving (26.5 g) / 100 = 11.9, rounded to 12.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 106 mg/100g — well below the 200 mg/100g threshold for solid foods
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is low at 106 mg/100g; however, cumulative sodium intake should be monitored when consuming large bowl servings (250 g = ~265 mg sodium), and added sugar should be minimised as excess sugar intake can independently elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 8.9 g/100g with low dietary fibre 0.7 g/100g; estimated GI 45 (low) partially mitigated by acetic acid in black vinegar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions (no more than 150 g per meal), pair it with high-fibre vegetables to blunt glucose response, and request reduced sugar in preparation, as the sugar-to-fibre ratio is unfavourable for blood glucose management despite the vinegar-mediated GI attenuation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork trotter is rich in collagen and saturated fat; estimated saturated fat ~1.6 g/100g with total fat 4.7 g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150 g or less, as the saturated fat from pork trotter skin and connective tissue contributes to LDL cholesterol load; the collagen-rich gelatinous matrix may have neutral-to-modest cardiovascular benefit, but the added sugar and pork fat combination warrants moderation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways in rheumatoid arthritis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish, as the arachidonic acid content of pork trotter promotes inflammatory cascades; the anti-inflammatory gingerols and acetic acid in the braising liquid provide insufficient counterbalance to the pro-inflammatory primary ingredient.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Vinegar Pig's Trotter (黑醋猪脚 / 黑醋豬腳) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese braised dish consisting of pig trotter pieces slow-cooked in a rich soup made from Chinese black vinegar (Zhenjiang or Fujian-style), fresh ginger, and rock sugar. The dish is deeply associated with postpartum confinement (坐月子) in Chinese culture, where it is traditionally prepared for new mothers to aid recovery, replenish blood, and warm the body. The trotter becomes gelatinous and tender through prolonged braising, with the collagen-rich skin and connective tissue dissolving into the dark, tangy-sweet braising liquid. The flavour profile is a complex balance of sour, sweet, and savoury, with a pronounced ginger warmth and the characteristic mellow acidity of aged black vinegar. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 107 kcal, 5.4 g protein, 4.7 g fat, 10.6 g carbohydrate (8.9 g sugar), 0.7 g dietary fibre, 106 mg sodium, and 62 mg calcium. The calcium content reflects the bone-in preparation and prolonged acid-assisted extraction from the trotter bones. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), as the acetic acid in black vinegar attenuates postprandial glucose response. Clinically, the dish is low in sodium but moderate in concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to added sugar and low fibre, and moderate for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from pork skin. It is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of pork. The dish is not halal. It is a culturally significant comfort food and medicinal dish in Singaporean Chinese households.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06100005) at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The GI is an analogous estimate (confidence medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on vinegar-mediated GI attenuation; this is the only component below high confidence. Overall record confidence is high given the exact HPB source match for all macros and secondary nutrients.
notes: saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.6 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central reference for braised pork trotter (NDB No. 10219) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately; this is an adapted value and should be verified against a direct HPB fatty acid panel if available. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific black vinegar pig's trotter preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F06100005, and no sufficiently close equivalent entry was found in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026-04-16. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is not halal as it contains pork. The input dietary_tags 'low_calorie' and 'low_sodium' are retained as contextually accurate at 107 kcal/100g and 106 mg sodium/100g respectively. The 'soft_foods' tag is appropriate given the gelatinous braised texture of the trotter.
